title: CVV Brute-Force Validation with Incremental Amount Testing
narrative: Fraudster systematically tests CVV codes on a stolen card PAN by cycling through common three-digit combinations (000, 001, 111, 123) using small-value transactions. Initial attempts show 4-8 CVV decline codes (05 or 82) across $1.00-$3.00 authorization requests within 6-12 minutes. After successful CVV match, fraudster immediately escalates to $247-$899 purchases, often in different MCC categories. Pattern shows card1_txn_count_1hr jumping from 0 to 6-9, all transactions showing identical card1/addr1 but rotating P_emaildomain across free providers. Successful CVV unlock typically occurs at attempt 3-7, followed by 2-4 higher-value approvals within 15 minutes. TransactionAmt shows clear two-tier structure: probe tier under $5, exploitation tier $200+. Frequently targets digital goods (ProductCD='W') or gift cards post-validation.
indicators: 4-8 consecutive declines with CVV mismatch response codes within 6-12 minute window
TransactionAmt under $5.00 during decline phase, then jumps to $200-$900 after first approval
card1_txn_count_1hr increases from 0 to 6+ transactions in single session
P_emaildomain changes across attempts while card1 and addr1 remain constant
Time delta between final decline and first high-value approval under 3 minutes
Successful transaction shows ProductCD shift to high-liquidity categories (W, C, or gift card MCC 5817)
All attempts originate from same dist1 value indicating identical device fingerprint
summary: A card shows 4-8 rapid declines with CVV error codes on small $1-$3 transactions, then immediately switches to high-value $200-$900 purchases once a valid CVV is found. Email addresses rotate across free providers while the card number and address stay identical.
reasoning: The attacker is systematically guessing CVV codes on a stolen card number until finding the correct combination, then immediately cashing out with large purchases before the card is frozen. The email rotation and small-to-large amount jump reveal deliberate testing rather than a legitimate customer making normal purchase mistakes.
indicator_explanations: Attacker is systematically testing CVV codes until they crack the correct one
Initial transactions are penny tests followed by immediate high-value fraud purchase
Card suddenly bursts from dormant to multiple transactions in under an hour
Email rotates between attempts while card and address stay locked during testing
Fraudster pivots to large purchase within minutes of finding valid CVV code
Approved transaction targets easily liquidated items like gift cards or crypto
All testing attempts come from the same device throughout the attack sequence
